*Name:* Lorrha Priory
*Country:* ireland
*Region:* the heartlands
*County:* County Tipperary
*Type:* abbey
*Period:* 6th-15th century
*Coords:* 53.0922, -8.1235
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lorrha
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Lorrha%20Priory/@52.957,-8.086,15z

*Gallery:* 4 images
*Image Source:* CDN
*Has Wikipedia:* no
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* needs more 

*Description:*
Lorrha is a small village at the northern tip of County Tipperary, Ireland. Ledewich described village as 'Larah' which is the common name for the talk-motes, mote hills etc. P. Dixon Hardy M.R.I.A. describes it as 'Loragh'

*Name:* Shane Castle
*Country:* ireland
*Region:* ulster and the north
*County:* County Antrim
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 16th-19th century
*Coords:* 54.2367, -6.8767
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shane%27s_Castle
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Shane%20Castle/@54.707,-6.31,15z
*Main Image:* https://img.castlecore.uk/shane-castle.jpg
*Gallery:* 4 images
*Image Source:* CDN
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Castleshane is a small village on the outskirts of Monaghan town in the north of County Monaghan in Ireland. The village is situated on the N2, the main road from Dublin to Derry and Letterkenny, and is located approximately 6 kilometres (3.7 mi) from Monaghan town and 17 km (11 mi) from Castleblayney. It is also located approximately 3 km (1.9 mi) away from the border with County Armagh, which is part of Northern Ireland.

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Kilmore Cathedral
*Country:* ireland
*Region:* ulster and the north
*County:* County Cavan
*Type:* abbey
*Period:* 12th century/19th century
*Coords:* 53.97971, -7.41469694
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/St_Fethlimidh's_Cathedral%2C_Kilmore
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Kilmore%20Cathedral/@53.973,-7.303,15z
*Main Image:* https://img.castlecore.uk/kilmore-cathedral.jpg
*Gallery:* 4 images
*Image Source:* CDN
*Has Wikipedia:* no
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* google places link - https://maps.app.goo.gl/CsxJDNygLAKC1h3X6


*Description:*
St Fethlimidh's Cathedral, Kilmore, is one of two cathedral churches in the Diocese of Kilmore, Elphin and Ardagh in the Church of Ireland. It is situated in the parish of Kilmore, southwest of the county town of Cavan. The name Kilmore - Cill Mhor meaning 'the great church' - reflects an earlier prominence that the Annals of the Four Masters have traced to an early medieval foundation. Of that church there are no physical remains

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Clones Round Tower
*Country:* ireland
*Region:* ulster and the north
*County:* County Monaghan
*Type:* abbey
*Period:* 10th-12th century
*Coords:* 54.183, -7.2337
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Clones_Abbey
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Clones%20Round%20Tower/@54.18,-7.229,15z
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Clones_Round_Tower_and_Graveyard.jpg/330px-Clones_Round_Tower_and_Graveyard.jpg
*Gallery:* 4 images
*Image Source:* wikipedia
*Has Wikipedia:* no
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Clones Abbey is a ruined monastery that later became an Augustinian abbey in the twelfth century, and its main sights are ecclesiastical. It was formerly known as St. Tighernach Abbey, and was referred to locally as the "wee abbey". Parochial and monastic settlements were separated, and it seems likely that the building became the Abbey of St. Peter and Paul. In the Book of Armagh and Annals of Ulster the word Clones is referenced as "Clauin Auis" and "Cluain Auiss," respectively. As there is no word in standard dictionaries of Old Irish that give the form "auis" or "eois", Seosamh Ó Dufaigh has speculated that the word is a cognate of the Welsh word for point or a tip: "awch". Bearnard O'Dubhthaigh disputes this theory on the grounds that the earlier form of "awch" is "afwch". Folklore suggests that the monastic town was originally called "Cluin Innish" due to being surrounded by water.

_Listing 4 of 204_

listing.name: Clones Round Tower
listing.desc: Clones Abbey is a ruined monastery that later became an Augustinian abbey in the twelfth century, and its main sights are ecclesiastical. It was formerly known as St. Tighernach Abbey, and was referred to locally as the "wee abbey". Parochial and monastic settlements were separated, and it seems likely that the building became the Abbey of St. Peter and Paul. In the Book of Armagh and Annals of Ulster the word Clones is referenced as "Clauin Auis" and "Cluain Auiss," respectively. As there is no word in standard dictionaries of Old Irish that give the form "auis" or "eois", Seosamh Ó Dufaigh has speculated that the word is a cognate of the Welsh word for point or a tip: "awch". Bearnard O'Dubhthaigh disputes this theory on the grounds that the earlier form of "awch" is "afwch". Folklore suggests that the monastic town was originally called "Cluin Innish" due to being surrounded by water.

🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Christchurch Castle
*Country:* england
*Region:* south west
*County:* Dorset
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 12th century
*Coords:* 50.733369, -1.77501
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Christchurch_Castle
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Christchurch%20Castle/@50.7347,-1.7775,15z
*Main Image:* none
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* unknown
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* update google places link - https://maps.app.goo.gl/KmEGLGPyRnSupQ9c6


*Description:*
Christchurch Castle is a Norman motte and bailey castle in Christchurch, Dorset, England. The earliest stonework has been dated to 1160. The castle's site is inside the old Saxon burh dominating the River Avon's lowest crossing.

_Listing 7 of 204_

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Lydford Castle
*Country:* england
*Region:* south west
*County:* Devon
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 12th century
*Coords:* 50.6428, -4.1115
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lydford_Castle
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Lydford%20Castle/@50.6443,-4.0656,15z
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZMB5noTdNBHfAqV0R9M5jjzUcLTtHqkoBens_lr0rieksPpCSckbNqzd8ecv1SADMFq_k0FmD4GA583AMUPapUf90S9lBKWOoXi7rxe6LWYOk_ooDnJ_bvBnXwI7WlV9odEbv1P4zfLnn8RDQ=s4800-w1200
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Lydford Castle is a medieval castle in the town of Lydford, Devon, England. The first castle in Lydford, sometimes termed the Norman fort, was a small ringwork built in a corner of the Anglo-Saxon fortified burh in the years after the Norman conquest of England. It was intended to help control Devon following the widespread revolt against Norman rule in 1068. The Norman fort had been abandoned by the middle of the 12th century.

_Listing 8 of 204_

It was intended to help control Devon following the widespread revolt against Norman rule in 1068. The Norman fort had been abandoned by the middle of the 12th century.

imageAttribution.description: Lydford: the castle A relatively small ruin next door to the church, this roofless castle is set on a pleasant grassy mound.
